steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb meaning 'to rise' into a noun meaning 'sky' by adding a long 'aa' sound and hamza at the end
Adds conjunction 'and' to connect to previous phrase
Adds genitive case ending to show possession or after certain prepositions

steps.m: and the sky (possessed by/connected to what follows)
steps.n: Adds a special ending to show this word is connected to what comes after it
This is a special word that shows something has or possesses a quality. Here it's describing 'the sky' (السَّمَآءِ) to show what the sky has.

steps.m: possessing/having (describes السَّمَآءِ and connects to ٱلْبُرُوجِ)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word connects what comes before it (السَّمَآءِ - the sky) with what comes after it (ٱلْبُرُوجِ - the constellations/mansions).

steps.m: to be elevated, rise up high

steps.n: Root letters were connected with vowels to make a noun meaning a tall structure
Changed vowels and added و to make it plural, showing multiple constellations
Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end because it follows ذَاتِ (possessing/having)

steps.m: a day (owned by الموعود - the promised)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic noun
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific day

steps.m: of the day (owned by الموعود - the promised)
steps.n: Adding kasra (-i) at the end shows this word is connected to something after it, like saying 'of the day'

steps.m: and of the day (owned by الموعود - the promised)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this word to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'to promise' into 'something that was promised' - like changing 'gift' to 'gifted thing'
Added ال to specify we're talking about a particular promised thing (in this case, the Day of Judgment mentioned in the ayah)
Added kasra to show this describes 'the day' (الْيَوْمِ) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: to witness or see something happen

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show someone who does the action, like changing 'witness' to 'witness-er'
Added double kasra to show it's indefinite (like saying 'a witness' instead of 'the witness') and affected by the word before it
Added وَ at the beginning to connect it to the previous word, like adding 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding مَ at start and changing vowels makes it describe what receives the action of witnessing (like changing 'see' to 'seen')
The tanwin (double kasra) shows it's indefinite and pairs with the word وَشَاهِدٍ (and a witness) in the ayah

steps.m: and a witnessed thing (paired with وَشَاهِدٍ)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects it to وَشَاهِدٍ (and a witness) that came before it, making a pair: 'a witness and something witnessed'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from active 'he killed' to passive 'was killed/destroyed' by changing vowels. Used as a curse against the people of the ditch (أَصْحٰبُ ٱلْأُخْدُودِ)

steps.m: to be a companion, accompany closely

steps.n: Added ا after first letter and ِ after second letter to create the pattern for someone who does the action
Changed to broken plural form by adding أَ at start and changing middle vowel to ٰ. This is a special plural form in Arabic.
The ُ ending shows this word is the subject - it tells us who was cursed/killed in the phrase قُتِلَ أَصْحٰبُ (the companions were cursed/killed)

steps.m: to dig, to make a deep cut in the ground

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The word changes from 'he dug' to 'it was dug' by changing the first vowel mark to a 'u' sound (like in 'put')
Changes the word into a noun meaning 'a deep trench' by adding أُ at the start and و before the last letter
Adding 'al' makes the word specific, like adding 'the' in English
The ِ ending shows this word belongs to أَصْحٰبُ (companions). Together they form the phrase 'companions of the trench'
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'fire'
Added 'the' (ال) at the start, which made the ن double in sound

steps.m: of the fire (described by ذَاتِ ٱلْوَقُودِ - that which has fuel)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the words that come after it, which describe what kind of fire it is
This is a special word used to show possession or attribution. It's feminine because it's describing النَّارِ which is feminine.

steps.m: of (connecting النَّارِ 'the fire' with الْوَقُودِ 'the fuel')
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word connects النَّارِ (the fire) with الْوَقُودِ (the fuel), making it mean 'the fire of fuel'
Added vowel marks and و after second root letter to create noun meaning 'fuel'

steps.m: the fuel (possessed by/of النَّارِ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite and ِ to show it belongs to 'the Fire' mentioned before it in the ayah

steps.m: when (referring to a past time, followed by هُمْ عَلَيْهَا قُعُودٌ)
steps.n: This is a time word that shows something happened in the past. In this ayah, it introduces the scene 'when they were sitting over it'
This is a pronoun that refers to a group of males who are mentioned in the ayah. It's the subject of قُعُودٌ (sitting), showing who is doing the sitting.
This is a common preposition showing something is on top of something else. In this ayah, it's describing where people are sitting.
The ي with sukun (ْ) is added to prepare for attaching the pronoun

steps.m: on/upon it/her (referring to the fire mentioned earlier)
steps.n: The ها at the end refers back to something feminine mentioned before (the fire). It shows what is being sat upon.
This is the basic meaning of sitting or remaining seated
Changed the word to show the action of sitting done by multiple people together

steps.m: sitting (as a group, subject of هُمْ)
steps.n: Added tanween damma to show this is the subject of the pronoun هُمْ (they) in the ayah

steps.m: they (who are witnessing what is being done to the believers)
steps.n: This is a basic pronoun meaning 'they' for a group of males. In this ayah, it refers to the people witnessing what is being done to the believers.

steps.m: and they (who are witnessing what is being done to the believers)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it, meaning 'and'
This is the basic form of the word that carries the meaning of being high or elevated
Adding ى changes this from a concept of height into a preposition showing position above something
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is the final form. In this ayah, it shows they are witnesses over/upon what the believers do.
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which'. In this ayah, it's the object of يَفْعَلُونَ (they do), showing what is being done to the believers.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a complete word
Changed to present tense with ي at start, sukun on first root letter, and damma on last root letter

steps.m: they do/act (referring to the disbelievers watching the believers)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the disbelievers who are watching what they do to the believers.
This is the basic past tense form of the verb
Past tense verb pattern فَعِلَ showing 'he' did the action of feeling safe/trusting
Adding مُ at the start and changing middle letter to ؤ turns 'he trusted' into 'one who trusts/believes' - this is a special pattern for this important word
The ين ending changes the word from 'one believer' to 'many believers'. Like adding 's' in English, but it also shows these believers are being affected by something in the sentence.
Adding 'ال' shows we're talking about specific believers, not just any believers
The letter ب (bi) is added at the start to mean 'with' or 'by' the believers

steps.m: to witness or observe something directly
steps.n: Basic past tense form meaning one person witnessed
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from a past tense verb (he witnessed) to a plural noun (witnesses) by changing vowels and adding و

steps.m: witnesses (who are watching what is being done to the believers)
steps.n: Added tanween (double damma) to show this is an indefinite noun. In this ayah, these witnesses are watching what is being done to the believers.
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context. In this ayah, it means 'not' because it's being used with a negative meaning.

steps.m: and not (connects this negation to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negation to what came before it

steps.m: to hate or seek revenge against something/someone

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to make the past tense verb 'he hated'. Works with وَمَا to mean 'and he did not hate'
Adding وا shows multiple people (they) did the action. This group is the subject of وَمَا (and not) in this ayah.
Combination of من (from) + هم (them)
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning of exception
Added a long alif (آ) at the end to stretch the sound, connecting it to the next phrase about belief. This emphasizes that belief was the only reason for persecution.
This is a connecting word that links the previous part of the verse to what happens next. It shows that what follows (they believe) is what was being criticized.
Added Form IV pattern which makes the meaning more active and transitive
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start. The hamza changes form due to pronunciation rules.
Changed to plural 'they' form and subjunctive mood because it follows أَن (that) in the ayah
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one true deity, making it a proper name
Added fatha on the shadda for proper pronunciation
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection
Added بِ at the beginning to show how something is done or the means by which it happens. Also added the connecting alif (ٱ) that comes after بِ.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show a strong quality
Added 'the' to make it specific and show it's referring to Allah
Added kasra at the end to show this describes Allah in the ayah
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes from 'he praised' (a verb) to 'praiseworthy' (a describing word) by using the pattern fa'eel
Makes the word specific by adding 'the' at the start
Shows this word is connected to the word before it
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to what they're describing
Added ال with shadda to make it specific and connect it to what comes after. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who owns the dominion of the heavens and earth.
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning
Added the pronoun ه with damma to show possession. This word shows that مُلْكُ (dominion) belongs to him.
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: dominion/kingdom (possessed by لَهُۥ - to him belongs)
steps.n: Changed the root letters into a noun meaning the thing or area that is ruled or possessed. This word is connected to لَهُۥ (to him belongs) in this ayah.

steps.m: dominion/kingdom (subject of لَهُۥ - to him belongs)
steps.n: Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this word is the subject of لَهُۥ (to him belongs)
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (rose up) to a noun (sky) by adding ء at the end - sky is named this because it is above us
Added وات to make it plural, showing there are multiple heavens
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the س (shown by ّ) because it comes after ٱل
Changed the way 'heavens' is written with long aa marks (ٰ) and added 'i' sound at end to show it belongs to something
Basic root letters got vowel marks to make the word 'earth'
Added ٱلْ to make it specific
Added وَ to connect it with the previous word (heavens)
This is a unique proper name that doesn't come from a root. It's the personal name of God in Islam.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is the subject doing the action in this sentence. Here, Allah is the one who is شَهِيدٌ (witness) over everything.

steps.m: and Allah (as the subject of شَهِيدٌ)
steps.n: The وَ at the beginning connects this to what came before, while ٱ is just how the ال looks after وَ.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else
The small alif (ىٰ) at the end shows this is connected to what comes after it. Here it connects to كُلِّ شَىْءٍ (everything) that Allah is witness over.
This is a particle that doesn't come from a root
Connected the letters and added damma (ُ) on first letter and shadda (ّ) on second letter to show totality
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word connects to the next word شَىْءٍ (thing), meaning 'of every thing'
Added fatha on ش and sukoon on ي to make it mean 'a thing'. The hamza at the end comes from the root letter أ.
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this thing is being possessed by كُلِّ (every/all). The ي is written as ى when it comes before hamza.

steps.m: one who witnesses/observes (describing Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters and kasra on middle letter to show someone who does this action as a constant quality. In this ayah, it's describing Allah as the One who witnesses everything.
The double-damma shows this is one of Allah's names/attributes being used independently
This is the basic form of the word that shows something might happen
Adding the shadda changes this from a word about possibility to one about certainty. It makes what comes after it stronger and more definite.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ال with a shadda to make it more specific

steps.m: those who (referring to multiple people who persecuted the believers)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word refers to the persecutors mentioned in this ayah who tortured the believing men and women.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: they tested/persecuted (subject of لَمْ يَتُوبُوا - did not repent)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show a group of people did this action. This refers to those who tested/persecuted the believers but didn't repent.
Adding vowels makes it a past tense verb about feeling secure
Changed the word to show who does the action instead of the action itself
Added ين to show we're talking about many believers

steps.m: the believers (referring to a specific group of believers mentioned in this verse)
steps.n: Added ال to point to specific believers and َ at the end because this word is affected by إِنَّ at the start of the verse
Added مُ at the start and changed middle letter to make it mean 'someone who believes'
Added ات to make it refer to multiple women who believe
Added وَ (and) to connect this word to what came before it
Added ال to point to specific female believers mentioned in this context

steps.m: and the female believers (object of فَتَنُوا)
steps.n: Added ِ to show these believers are the ones being persecuted by the disbelievers in فَتَنُوا (they persecuted)
Basic form of the word that shows one thing happens after another

steps.m: then/thereafter (connecting فَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ with what came before)
steps.n: The word 'then' gets dots above (ُ َ) and a double letter mark (ّ) to show it connects two ideas together - like saying 'this happened, then that happened'
Basic negative particle that shows something didn't happen
Adding مْ makes it specifically about things that didn't happen in the past. Here it works with يَتُوبُوا۟ (repent) to say 'did not repent'

steps.m: he returned back (to what is right)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make past tense verb. The middle و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: he returns back (to what is right)
steps.n: Changes from past to present by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowels

steps.m: they return back (to what is right)
steps.n: Makes the verb plural masculine for 'they'. This word appears after لَمْ (not) in the ayah, so it means 'they did not repent'.
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to

steps.m: then for them (connected to عَذَابُ - punishment)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. This word connects to عَذَابُ (punishment) in the ayah, showing what they will get.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

Added alif after second letter to make it a noun meaning 'punishment'. The U ending shows this word owns something or is the subject. Here it's connected to جَهَنَّمَ (Hell) to mean 'Hell's punishment'.
This is a proper noun for the place of punishment in the afterlife. It doesn't come from an Arabic root because it's a borrowed word.
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being possessed - the punishment OF Hell. It connects to عَذَابُ (punishment) in the phrase عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ (punishment of Hell).
This is a basic particle showing direction or purpose
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it

steps.m: and for them (connected to عَذَابُ - punishment)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this is for 'them' (referring to those who persecuted the believers mentioned earlier in the verse)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (he punished) to a noun (punishment) by adding alif. Shows it's not just any punishment but the strong punishment of Hell.
Adds the nominative case ending ُ to show this word is the subject of فَلَهُمْ (then for them is)
Added ي between second and third root letters to show intensity of burning
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it describes عَذَابُ (punishment) in the phrase 'punishment of the blaze'
This is the basic form of the word that shows a condition or possibility
Adding the shadda changes this from a conditional word to one that adds emphasis. It affects the next word ٱلَّذِينَ (those who) by making it take a special ending.

steps.m: those who - connects descriptions to people or things
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun used to describe groups of people. The ين ending shows it's plural masculine.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added a long alif to change the meaning to actively believing rather than just trusting

steps.m: they believed (connected to وَعَمِلُوا۟ - and they did)
steps.n: Added وا to show many people did this action. This word connects to وَعَمِلُوا۟ (and they did) in the ayah, showing the same group doing both actions.
Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels

steps.m: they worked/performed deeds (connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ - believed)
steps.n: Plural masculine past tense suffix indicating 'they (masculine)'. This refers to the same group who believed in the previous word ءَامَنُوا۟.
وَ means 'and' - it connects this action to the previous one
Added vowels to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
Changes the verb into a word describing someone or something that has this quality
Makes it plural feminine to refer to multiple good deeds or actions
Adds 'the' at the beginning and shows this word is being acted upon in the phrase 'they did righteous deeds'
This is the basic preposition that shows something is for someone or to someone

steps.m: for them (referring to those who believe and do good deeds)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who the preposition is referring to. In this ayah, it refers back to those who believe and do good deeds.

steps.m: to cover or hide with vegetation

steps.n: Added vowels and connected the letters together. The doubled ن (shadda) shows intensity of covering.
Added ة to make it a noun for a place that is covered with vegetation
Changed to plural form by replacing ة with ات
Added ٌ (double damma) to show this is the subject of لَهُمْ (for them), telling us what belongs to the believers
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added تَ at start to make it present tense (happening now)
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it shows that the rivers flow starting from beneath the gardens.
Connected the letters together with vowel marks to make it pronounceable
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by gardens (جَنَّاتٌ)
Added هَا (meaning 'its/her') to show these things are under the gardens
Added vowel marks to make the basic singular noun for river
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple rivers
Added 'the' (ال) to make it specific rivers, and the ending ُ shows it connects to gardens as what flows beneath them
Basic demonstrative word meaning 'this' for something close by
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's a demonstrative word
Adding لِ changes it from pointing to something close ('this') to something far away ('that')

steps.m: that (referring to the gardens mentioned before)
steps.n: The كَ at the end makes it more specific, pointing back to something just mentioned (in this case, the gardens of Paradise)
Changed from action to noun meaning the result of achieving success. This is part of ذٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (that is the great success) which describes the reward mentioned before.

steps.m: the success (part of ذٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ - that is the great success)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific/definite. The ُ shows it's part of the nominal sentence ذٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (that is the great success) describing the rewards mentioned in the previous part of the verse.
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a permanent and complete quality. When used for Allah, it means His greatness is absolute and perfect.

steps.m: The Great One (a name of Allah)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it definite like 'the' in English. The ُ shows this is one of Allah's names standing alone. This is one of Allah's 99 beautiful names (Al-Kabeer), describing His absolute greatness above all creation.
This is the basic form of the word that shows uncertainty or condition
Adding shadda changes this from a word of uncertainty to one of certainty. It emphasizes the phrase that follows it: 'indeed, the grip/punishment of your Lord is severe'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun (verbal noun) that means 'the act of seizing'. This noun is owned by رَبِّكَ (your Lord) and is emphasized by إِنَّ (indeed) at the start of the phrase.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop to completion
Lord (the one who nurtures and cares completely)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows intensity in the meaning
Added كَ at the end to show it belongs to 'you' (if speaking to one man). This word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by/belongs to بَطْشَ (the assault/grip).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from an action (becoming strong) to a describing word (strong/intense) that tells us about Allah's grip
Added two dots on top (double damma) to show this is a describing word in its basic form
Added لَ at start to emphasize that something is definitely strong/intense
This is a special word that makes statements stronger. It's like adding 'indeed' at the start of a sentence in English to show you're very sure about something.

steps.m: indeed he (referring to Allah in هُوَ يُبْدِئُ)
steps.n: Added هُۥ at the end to show who we're talking about. This connects to the next part of the ayah where it says هُوَ (He) talking about Allah.
This is a standalone pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who is the one who begins and repeats creation.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start. This word relates back to هُوَ (He) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters. The middle و becomes ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter).
Added يُ at start to show present tense and changed middle vowel to ِ. The ا changes to ي because of the kasra before it.
Added ُ at the end to show this is a complete statement (like a period in English)

steps.m: And he returns/brings back (connected to يُبْدِئُ - he begins)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this word to يُبْدِئُ (he begins) in the ayah, showing Allah both begins and returns things
This is a basic pronoun that means 'he'
The و at the start means 'and'. This word connects to the next words (ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلْوَدُودُ) to say 'and He is the Most Forgiving, the Most Loving'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who constantly covers up faults
steps.n: Changed vowels and added و to show this is someone who forgives again and again

steps.m: The Most Forgiving (one of Allah's names, subject of هُوَ)
steps.n: Added ال (the) to make it definite + damma ending since it describes Allah (after هُوَ/He)

steps.m: to love deeply and show affection

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The و after the second root letter and the ُ vowel pattern create an intensive form showing someone who does this action strongly or frequently

steps.m: The Most Loving (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific and definite. When used as one of Allah's names, it takes on the superlative meaning of 'Most'. The ُ at the end shows it's the subject of the sentence.
This is a special word that shows ownership or possession. It doesn't come from a root like most Arabic words.
Connected the letters to form the basic word. This word is special because it needs something after it to show what is owned/possessed. Here it connects to ٱلْعَرْشِ (the throne).
Added the damma (ُ) mark to show this word is the subject doing something in the sentence.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun referring to a throne
Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to a specific throne rather than any throne
The ِ shows this word belongs to or is connected to ذُو (owner/possessor of)
The root describes glory and nobility
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Adding ي between the second and third letters makes it an intensive adjective showing someone/something has a lot of this quality

steps.m: The Glorious One (describing ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ - Owner of the Throne)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific and definite. This word is describing Allah through the phrase 'Owner of the Throne'

steps.m: The Glorious One (describing ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ - Owner of the Throne)
steps.n: Adding ُ shows this is describing the subject (Owner of the Throne)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who does intensively (subject of يُرِيدُ)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (ع) with the long alif shows this is someone who does something a lot. This word is the subject of يُرِيدُ (he wills/wants) in this ayah.

steps.m: one who does intensively (subject of يُرِيدُ)
steps.n: The ٌ (double damma) was added at the end to show this is an indefinite doer of an action, like saying 'a doer' instead of 'the doer'
Starting with the basic particles that will combine: ل (for) and ما (what)

steps.m: for what/that which (connects to يُرِيدُ - he wills)
steps.n: Combined the particles and added vowel marks. This word connects to يُرِيدُ (he wills) to mean 'for whatever he wills'

steps.m: indeed, for what/that which (connects to يُرِيدُ - he wills)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to show this is definitely or surely true
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Added يُ at start to show present tense, middle letter و changed to ي to make it easier to say. From basic 'want' to 'is wanting'
This is a question particle that always comes at the start of a sentence to turn it into a yes/no question
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. This word turns the statement 'the story of the soldiers reached you' into the question 'has the story of the soldiers reached you?'
Added fatha marks to show past tense and make the word mean 'he came'

steps.m: he came to you (object of هَلْ/has)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end which means 'to you'. This word is being asked about by هَلْ (has) at the start of the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (telling) to a noun (the thing being told)
The damma ending shows this word is the subject of the sentence. It's connected to 'the armies' (ٱلْجُنُودِ) that comes after it.

steps.m: an army or group of soldiers
steps.n: Added vowels to the root letters to make a word meaning 'army' or 'soldiers'
Changed from single army to plural armies by changing the middle vowels to u-u (like book/books)
Added ال to show these are specific armies being talked about
Added kasra at the end because this word belongs to حَدِيثُ (story), showing it's 'the story of the armies'

steps.m: Pharaoh - the title of ancient Egyptian kings
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language. It doesn't have an Arabic root.
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this word is receiving the action in the sentence, like how 'him' receives action in 'I saw him'

steps.m: Thamud (the ancient Arabian tribe mentioned with فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: This is a proper noun referring to an ancient Arabian people mentioned in the Quran. It doesn't have an Arabic root.

steps.m: Thamud (as the object being acted upon, like فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: The fatha (َ) at the end shows this word is receiving the action in the sentence, just like Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنَ) before it
The و at the beginning means 'and', connecting this word to Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنَ) that came before it
This is a basic particle that changes the direction of speech or corrects what was said before
Added kasra at the end because it connects to the next word ٱلَّذِينَ (those who). This shows the words are linked together in meaning.
Starting with the basic demonstrative word and changing it to make it relative
Adding ال at the beginning and doubling the ذ makes it specifically mean 'the one who'
Adding ين at the end makes it plural, showing we're talking about multiple people. This word is describing the people who did the action كَفَرُوا۟ (they disbelieved)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. The small circle (۟) is just a pronunciation mark.
Basic preposition showing location or being inside something
The ى at the end shows this word means 'in' something specific that comes after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed into a noun that describes the action itself
Added tanwin kasra because it comes after the word فِى (in) in the ayah
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. The double ل with shadda is a special feature of this holy name.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Allah is the subject doing something in this sentence.
Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and'. The ٱ is just a helper letter that comes with ال.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'Allah' to 'behind them', showing Allah's position or perspective.

steps.m: to be behind or follow after
steps.n: The root letters show the basic concept of being behind something
Forms a spatial noun indicating position or direction from the root concept of being behind
The word changes form to show it will be possessed by something. Here it is possessed by هِم (them) to mean 'behind them'.
هِم is added to show 'their' or 'them'

steps.m: to surround or protect by enclosing
steps.n: The root conveys the idea of surrounding or encircling something
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to make it into a word describing someone/something that does the action
Added shadda on م to emphasize the meaning
Added tanween to show this is a complete thought, describing Allah's attribute of completely surrounding and encompassing all things

steps.m: Rather (introduces هُوَ قُرْءَانٌ مَّجِيدٌ - it is a glorious Quran)
steps.n: This is a word that introduces a new statement while correcting what came before. Here it introduces 'it is a glorious Quran' to correct any wrong ideas.
This is a personal pronoun used to refer to a masculine singular thing. In this ayah, it refers back to the Quran.
This is the basic meaning from which 'Quran' is derived
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changes verb 'read' into a noun meaning 'that which is read' with emphasis on its recitation nature
Added tanwin to show this is describing 'it' (هُوَ) in the phrase 'but it is a glorious recitation'
This root conveys the idea of glory and nobility
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third letters to show this is a strong quality
Added double damma mark to show this is indefinite
The ّ doubles the م sound to add emphasis, like saying 'truly' or 'very'

steps.m: in (location of لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ - a preserved tablet)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing location. In this ayah, it shows that something is located in/within a preserved tablet.

steps.m: in (location of لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ - a preserved tablet)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) vowel mark to show how the word is pronounced

steps.m: in (location of لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ - a preserved tablet)
steps.n: Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation - it doesn't change the meaning
The root letters form a basic noun pattern
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make a noun meaning 'tablet' - something that displays or shows writing clearly
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this is an indefinite noun (like 'a' or 'an' in English). This tablet is described as preserved (مَّحْفُوظٍۭ) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the word to show what receives the protection rather than who does the protecting
The doubled م makes the meaning stronger - like saying 'really protected'
Added tanween kasra to show this describes 'لَوْحٍ' (tablet) from earlier in the verse
